mnemonics.title: ML Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: S.U.R. - Supervised Uses kNowledge (labeled), Unsupervised fRomself (no labels), Reinforcement Receives rewards/penalties after actions.

mnemonics.title: Overfitting vs Underfitting
mnemonics.content: OVERFITTING = Too much detail (memorizing), UNDERFITTING = Too simple (guessing). Think: Overfit = OVer-remember, Underfit = Under-learn.

mnemonics.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
mnemonics.content: HIGH BIAS = Underfitting (too simple), HIGH VARIANCE = Overfitting (too complex). Balance is key for good generalization.

mnemonics.title: Classification vs Regression
mnemonics.content: CLASSify = Discrete categories (spam/not spam), REGress = Continuous values (price, temperature).

acronyms.usage: The field of study enabling computers to learn from data without explicit programming.
Broader field where ML is a subset; aims to create intelligent machines.
ML using neural networks with multiple hidden layers.
Computing system inspired by biological neural networks, fundamental to DL.
Dimensionality reduction technique used in preprocessing.
Technique to assess how model generalizes to independent datasets.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a team hierarchy: Input Layer = Receptionist (receives data), Hidden Layers = Managers (process/transform), Output Layer = CEO (final decision/prediction).
Think of a flexible rubber sheet that exactly fits training points (overfit) vs a rigid board that captures general trend (good fit).
Picture target shooting: High bias = shots clustered away from bullseye (consistently wrong), High variance = shots scattered everywhere (inconsistent).
Raw data = uncut diamonds; Feature engineering = cutting and polishing to reveal valuable insights.

analogies.analogy: Like teaching a child to recognize animals: show many pictures (training data), and eventually the child learns to identify new animals (predictions) without explicit rules.
Like learning with a teacher who provides answers (labels) for practice problems, helping you learn the pattern.
Like organizing a messy room without labels - you group similar items together based on their characteristics.
Like training a dog with treats: good actions get rewards, bad actions get scolded; the dog learns optimal behavior over time.
Like choosing the right ingredients and prepping them before cooking - quality ingredients (features) lead to better dishes (predictions).
